title: Design a Blog Post API
problemStatement: Design a RESTful API for a blogging platform. Users can create, read, update, and delete blog posts. Posts can have tags, and readers can leave comments. Support listing posts with pagination and tag filtering.
functionalRequirements: List posts with pagination and tag filtering
Add and list comments on a post
Each post has a title, content, author, tags, and timestamps
constraints: Only the post author can update or delete a post
Deleting a post also deletes its comments

rubric.reasoning: Seven endpoints cover the full blog lifecycle. Nested routes for comments (/posts/:id/comments) clearly express the parent-child relationship. Flat routes (/comments?postId=X) work too but are less discoverable.
POST creates (new post, new comment), GET reads, PUT updates (full replace), DELETE removes. Using POST for everything is the most common mistake — it destroys HTTP's built-in semantic guarantees.

This challenge uniquely needs 403 — editing someone else's post. The difference between 401 (not logged in) and 403 (logged in but not the author) is critical for the client to show the right error message.

Authorization-based errors (403 for non-authors) are the most nuanced here. The server must check: is the user authenticated? → is this post theirs? → is the update valid? Each layer has a different error code.
Reading posts should be public (no auth needed for GET). Creating, updating, and deleting require auth. This asymmetry is common in content APIs — maximize read access, protect write access.
Blog posts grow unboundedly — pagination is mandatory. Consider cursor-based pagination for feeds (chronological order) and offset for admin views (jump to page).
Tag filtering uses ?tag=javascript to narrow results. Sorting uses ?sort=-createdAt for newest-first (the minus prefix convention). These compose: ?tag=react&sort=-createdAt gives newest React posts.

defendQuestions.question: You used /posts/:id/comments for comments. What are the tradeoffs vs a flat route like /comments?postId=X?
defendQuestions.modelReasoning: Nested routes (/posts/:id/comments) make the relationship explicit — comments belong to a post. Benefits: clearer URL structure, automatic validation that the post exists, authorization can cascade. Flat routes (/comments?postId=X) are better when comments need to be queried independently (e.g., 'show me all comments by user Y across all posts'). Most APIs use nested for creation and listing under a parent, but add a flat /comments endpoint for cross-resource queries.

defendQuestions.question: When a post is deleted, should its comments also be deleted? What are the implications?
defendQuestions.modelReasoning: Cascade delete (delete post → delete all comments) is simplest but permanently destroys user-generated content. Alternative: soft-delete the post (mark as deleted) and hide comments. Another option: orphan the comments (keep them but remove the post reference). For a blog, cascade delete is usually fine since comments are contextually meaningless without the post. For a platform where comments have independent value (like Stack Overflow answers), cascade delete would be destructive.

defendQuestions.question: How do you handle the 'only the author can edit/delete' rule? Where does that check happen?
defendQuestions.modelReasoning: Middleware checks authentication (is the token valid?), then the route handler checks authorization (does this user own this post?). The check is: fetch the post → compare post.authorId with req.user.id → 403 if mismatch. Never trust client-side checks alone. For complex apps, consider a policy engine (like CASL or Casbin) that centralizes authorization rules instead of scattering if-checks across handlers.

endpoints.description: List all blog posts with pagination and filtering
Get a single post by ID
Add a comment to a post

designNotes: Use nested routes for comments (/posts/:id/comments) — comments belong to a post
403 Forbidden for editing/deleting posts you don't own (vs 401 for not logged in)
Tag filtering uses query params, not separate endpoints
Support sorting by date or title via ?sort=createdAt or ?sort=-createdAt for descending
